brief_document.title: Durable timers — schedule_timer and expired_timers
brief_document.purpose: Implement the durable timer substrate over the timers table: persist scheduled timers idempotently and retrieve those due to fire as of a given instant. This is what lets a workflow sleep for months and have the engine, on restart, discover which timers are due.
brief_document.task: Implement schedule_timer and expired_timers in timer.rs against the timers table from AS-002. schedule_timer upserts a TimerEntry keyed by (workflow_id, timer_id) so re-scheduling replaces (idempotent under replay). expired_timers(as_of) range-scans fire_at <= as_of. The store returns due timers; it does not fire them.

requirements.title: schedule_timer (idempotent upsert)
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL persist a durable timer keyed by (workflow_id, timer_id). WHEN the same timer id is scheduled again, THE SYSTEM SHALL replace the prior row rather than insert a duplicate, so scheduling is idempotent under replay.
acceptance: timer.rs implements schedule_timer(&self, &WorkflowId, &TimerId, fire_at: DateTime<Utc>) -> Result<(), StoreError> as an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(workflow_id, timer_id) DO UPDATE (upsert), writing fire_at as RFC3339.
Re-scheduling the same (workflow_id, timer_id) with a new fire_at updates the row to the new fire_at and does not create a second row.
A test schedules a timer, re-schedules it with a different fire_at, and asserts exactly one row exists with the latest fire_at.
libsql errors map to StoreError::Backend; serialisation of the timestamp is lossless and round-trips.

requirements.title: expired_timers (due-as-of range scan)
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL return all timers whose fire_at is less than or equal to a given instant, and SHALL NOT return timers whose fire_at is after that instant.
acceptance: timer.rs implements expired_timers(&self, as_of: DateTime<Utc>) -> Result<Vec<TimerEntry>, StoreError> as an indexed range scan on fire_at <= as_of.
Each returned TimerEntry carries workflow_id, timer_id, and fire_at reconstructed from the row.
A test schedules timers at distinct instants (past, exactly-as_of, future) and asserts expired_timers returns exactly the due ones (<= as_of), excluding the future one.
The boundary is inclusive at as_of (a timer with fire_at == as_of is returned), matching the AC-005/AC-006 contract; the test asserts the boundary case.

boundaries: SHALL NOT fire timers or schedule wall-clock callbacks — the store returns due timers; firing is the engine (AE) and durability/replay (AD).
SHALL NOT implement append/read/query — AS-004, AS-005.
SHALL NOT store timers in the events table — they live in the dedicated timers table from AS-002.
SHALL NOT create duplicate rows for a re-scheduled timer id.
verification: A reviewer confirms re-scheduling is an upsert (no duplicate rows) and the expired_timers boundary is inclusive at as_of.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: schedule_timer persists a TimerEntry into the timers table keyed by (workflow_id, timer_id); re-scheduling the same timer id replaces the prior row (idempotent under replay).
expired_timers(as_of) returns all timers with fire_at <= as_of and excludes timers not yet due.

stories.text: As the engine, I want to list active workflows and durable timers from storage on startup so that I know what to replay and which timers are due after downtime.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny. No unsafe in the crate.
No #[allow]/#[expect]/#[ignore] bypasses per CLAUDE.md. Tests needing a remote primary use a runtime env-gate, not #[ignore].
lib.rs is declarations and re-exports only; logic lives in named modules.
500-line file limit (excluding tests/comments/whitespace). The EventStore impl is split across append, read, and timer modules.
Depends only on aion-store (and transitively aion-core) among Aion crates — no dependency on the engine, beamr, or any other backend. Structural; must hold.
Raw SQL is permitted here because this crate IS a storage backend (the storage layer). No other crate issues SQL against this store; they go through the EventStore trait.
WorkflowStatus is derived from event history via aion-core's projection. No mutable stored status field; a cached projection must never diverge from the events.
LibSqlStore must pass the shared AC-007 conformance suite unmodified — the same behavioural standard as the oracle.
append's sequence guard and event inserts execute in a single libSQL transaction; a mismatch writes nothing and returns SequenceConflict — no partial batch is ever persisted.
LibSqlConfig is Deserialize-only and carries no hardcoded durability/WAL defaults; tunable values are surfaced for the operator to set, per CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults.
All libsql and serde errors are mapped into the StoreError taxonomy at the crate boundary; no foreign error type leaks across the EventStore surface.

resolved_context.intention: When an engine operator embeds Aion and writes no storage configuration, this is the store they get: a single file on disk, zero external infrastructure, full durability. The same crate serves the distributed path — the local file becomes an embedded replica syncing to a remote primary — without the operator learning a second backend. The in-memory reference store (cluster AC) proves the contract but forgets on restart; LibSqlStore makes durability the easy default, held to the identical behavioural standard as the oracle. A workflow that sleeps for months and resumes needs its history to survive every crash and reboot in between. When this cluster is done, an operator writes LibSqlStore::open("app.db") and has a production-grade durable event store the engine cannot tell apart from the oracle except that it remembers; the shared AC-007 conformance suite passes against it unchanged, plus a durability-specific suite proving state survives close-and-reopen.
